title: An Emerging Markets Knockout | Dimensional
gist: Taiwan and South Korea posted extraordinary one-year gains of 104% and 181%, driven by semiconductor leaders TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix, vaulting both into the world's ten largest stock markets and overtaking the UK. Dimensional uses this as evidence that broad diversification, not concentrated stock-picking, is the way to capture unpredictable market winners.

themes.theme: Taiwan and South Korea's market cap surge

insights.claim: Taiwan's stock market returned 104% over the one-year period ending June 30, 2026 (MSCI Taiwan IMI, net dividends, USD), pushing it past the UK to become the world's third-largest stock market.
insights.speaker: Matt Lipps, CFA, Investment Strategist
insights.so_what: A one-year 104% move in a major market signals a large valuation/positioning shift; investors underweight Taiwan (e.g., via developed-market-only or UK-heavy allocations) missed a top-10 global market re-ranking.
insights.quote: Taiwan is now the world’s third-largest stock market, overtaking the UK
insights.locator: Following a strong second quarter, Taiwan and South Korea have surged

insights.claim: South Korea's stock market returned 181% over the one-year period ending June 30, 2026 (MSCI Korea IMI, net dividends, USD), climbing to the sixth-largest stock market globally.
insights.speaker: Matt Lipps, CFA, Investment Strategist
insights.so_what: A 181% one-year return is an extreme outlier move for a national equity market; it illustrates how much upside can be missed by investors who exclude or underweight emerging/non-US markets.
insights.quote: South Korea have surged 104% and 181% over the past year
insights.locator: Following a strong second quarter, Taiwan and South Korea have surged

insights.claim: The Taiwan and South Korea rallies were driven largely by semiconductor leaders TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix.
insights.speaker: Matt Lipps, CFA, Investment Strategist
insights.so_what: Country-level index returns in Taiwan and Korea are effectively a semiconductor-cycle bet; investors should recognize concentration risk in these markets even though they show up as 'diversification' at the country-allocation level.
insights.quote: Much of this performance has been driven by semiconductor leaders such as TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix.
insights.locator: Much of this performance has been driven by semiconductor leaders

insights.claim: The rotation in market-cap rankings pushed the UK out of the top three global stock markets as Taiwan overtook it.
insights.speaker: Matt Lipps, CFA, Investment Strategist
insights.so_what: Global market-cap leadership is shifting away from legacy developed markets like the UK toward Asian tech/semiconductor-driven economies, a trend worth tracking for benchmark and index-construction implications.
insights.locator: Taiwan is now the world’s third-largest stock market, overtaking the UK

insights.claim: Dimensional frames the Taiwan/South Korea surge as proof that outsized market returns can emerge unexpectedly in any country, making broad diversification (holding both 'crowd favorites and underdogs') the way to reliably capture such gains.
insights.speaker: Matt Lipps, CFA, Investment Strategist
insights.so_what: Investors should maintain broad country/market exposure rather than concentrating in a handful of 'safe' developed markets, since the next big winner is hard to predict in advance.
insights.quote: Diversified portfolios that include crowd favorites and underdogs can allow investors to participate in good fortune wherever and whenever it appears.
insights.locator: Their remarkable returns are a reminder that strong outcomes

insights.claim: Dimensional implicitly criticizes selective, concentrated stock/market-picking strategies, arguing they risk missing major winners like Taiwan and South Korea's rally.
insights.speaker: Matt Lipps, CFA, Investment Strategist
insights.so_what: A veiled critique of active/concentrated stock-picking versus passive, broadly diversified indexing; useful framing when evaluating manager or portfolio construction choices that exclude emerging markets.
insights.quote: By contrast, investors who pick and choose might find themselves caught offside when the big moment arrives.
insights.locator: By contrast, investors who pick and choose

insights.claim: The article uses the World Cup knockout stage as a framework for market unpredictability: just as underdog teams can pull off upsets, underdog markets (Taiwan, South Korea) can suddenly outperform established heavyweights.
insights.speaker: Matt Lipps, CFA, Investment Strategist
insights.so_what: Reinforces a mental model for investors: treat market leadership as inherently unpredictable and design portfolios that don't require correctly forecasting which market or sector wins next.
insights.quote: As the World Cup enters the knockout stage, every match has the potential to produce an unexpected outcome.
insights.locator: As the World Cup enters the knockout stage
